petitioner_background: The petitioner is a physician specializing in musculoskeletal radiology, interventional radiology, and ultrasound. He trained at various medical schools in India and the United States, and is a board certified radiologist. Since 2012, he has worked for an employer in a U.S. territory (redacted). He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under INA § 203(b)(1)(A). A prior immigrant petition in a different classification was filed on his behalf in 2014 and approved in 2015.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner documented membership in seven medical associations and asserted that they require outstanding achievements of their members. As supporting references, he identified the websites of some associations and submitted bylaws for others. He argued that board certification is 'a demanding process' and that any membership requiring board certification is a qualifying membership, stating 'Being board certified in radiology is considered an outstanding achievement in the medical field' and 'Out of many medical doctors in the world, only a few percentages are board certified in radiology.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found one association's website did not address membership requirements at all, and materials from other associations listed requirements no more stringent than board certification, experience, member sponsorship (in some instances), and payment of dues or application fees. The AAO held that the petitioner did not establish that board certification is an outstanding achievement rather than a credential with rigorous requirements, nor did he establish the percentage of radiologists who are board certified — information crucial because if most radiologists are board certified, it cannot be a rare or special distinction. The AAO rejected the argument that reviewers of membership applications are nationally recognized simply by being board certified radiologists. The AAO also noted that most 'medical doctors' are not radiologists and therefore would have no reason to pursue board certification in radiology — one's choice of specialty is not intrinsically an outstanding achievement. The AAO further observed the petitioner inconsistently defined his field (sometimes as radiology, sometimes as 'medical field').

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of service on the editorial board of a Medical Association Journal (documented via email messages requesting peer review of manuscripts, dated after the petition's filing date), faculty review of student work, and participation in a peer review program at his institution in which 'each radiologist reviews other radiologists' work every quarter.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO (and Director) agreed the petitioner satisfied the letter of this criterion. However, in the final merits determination, the AAO found the judging activity consisted mainly of routine peer review. The emails documenting editorial board service were dated after filing and did not show pre-filing activity. The journal had published only one issue before the filing date, and the record did not show it enjoyed a level of prestige such that editorial board membership indicated recognition. Other judging (faculty review of student work, quarterly peer review at his institution) was routine and local. The petitioner himself acknowledged that 'Generally, an Assistant Professor at a medical school is required to assess and judge student coursework.' The AAO concluded this activity did not go beyond routine duties inherent in those positions.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner relied on his four published scholarly articles, arguing they 'are of major significance because the articles document work that is first of its kind.' His graduate thesis concerned bronchoscopy (a noninvasive imaging method of the respiratory system). Colleagues provided letters claiming that '[b]ecause of [the petitioner's] work in the field of bronchoscopy, it is now a routine noninvasive examination for diagnosing airway problems.' The petitioner documented 19 citations of one article and 5 citations of another, asserting his '4 articles have earned unusually high numbers of citations in the context of radiology.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO rejected the argument that originality alone equals major significance, noting the regulation requires contributions to be both 'original' and 'of major significance' — the latter clause would be redundant if originality inherently implied major significance. Because originality is a cornerstone requirement of scholarly publications generally, accepting the petitioner's reasoning would mean most published articles are contributions of major significance, contrary to the statutory demand for extensive documentation. The AAO also found it insufficient to show that the bronchoscopy technique is widely used; the petitioner had to establish it is widely used because of his work, especially since he did not claim to have invented it. The 19 citations did not establish the technique's adoption was attributable to him. The AAO found the petitioner's claim of 'continued to publish' misleading, since three of the four articles dated from 2003-04 with no further publications until 2019, shortly before filing. The 19 citations (over 15 years) and 5 citations (over 16 years) were not shown to be high relative to comparable articles — a search engine sample showed other articles with 32, 65, and 27 citations, and another article matched his 19 citations but over only 10 years.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of authorship of four scholarly articles, three dating from 2003-04 and one from 2019.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded, and the AAO agreed, that the petitioner satisfied this criterion. However, in the final merits determination, the AAO found that most of the petitioner's published articles derived from a time when he was completing his own medical training. The AAO rejected the argument that co-authoring 'the Medical Journal's first article ever' demonstrated distinction — there is no presumption of distinction for the inaugural issue of a new journal which, by definition, has no prior history of impact. The petitioner did not establish that only acclaimed radiologists produce published work or provide evidence meaningfully distinguishing his articles from others.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed critical roles for his employer (a radiology center) and a territorial medical association. He submitted Exhibit N, a 2018 press release announcing the center's 'three-year term of accreditation' for a particular medical procedure, stating that 'this specific accreditation has only been granted to 1,928 out of 40,517 accreditations in over 34 years.' He also contended the center 'is one of the highest-ranked U.S. radiology center[s] outside of the continental U.S.' Exhibit T was a letter from the executive director of the medical association stating the petitioner 'has been a very active member' of the organization, which is 'comprise[d] of medical doctors and allied health care providers.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO disagreed with the Director and found the petitioner did not satisfy this criterion. The petitioner did not submit objective evidence that accreditation is a mark of distinction rather than simply meeting certain professional standards, and did not show that this accreditation type is more prestigious than other types — the other accreditations may simply refer to other procedures. The claim that the center is 'one of the highest-ranked U.S. radiology center[s] outside of the continental U.S.' was unsubstantiated and necessarily excluded most U.S. radiology centers. Regarding the medical association, the letter did not establish it has a distinguished reputation in comparison with other state and territorial medical associations; 'distinguished' is inherently comparative, and territorial-level status does not inherently convey distinction. The AAO found the petitioner did not establish the association has a national or international reputation. Because neither organization was shown to have a distinguished reputation, the AAO did not reach whether the petitioner performed a leading or critical role.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted pay receipts and an employment contract showing his salary, along with Bureau of Labor Statistics data on median radiologist salaries.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director had erroneously concluded the petitioner's 'base salary' was much lower than total compensation, based on pay receipts that subtracted paid leave from hours worked. The AAO held that paid leave is not a fringe benefit in excess of base salary. The total pay shown on the pay receipts was consistent with the salary stated in the contract, and that total pay was substantially higher than the median salary shown in BLS data. The AAO therefore concluded the petitioner has commanded a high salary and satisfied this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: Although the petitioner satisfied three criteria (judging, scholarly articles, and high salary), the AAO determined in a final merits determination that the totality of the evidence did not establish sustained national or international acclaim or that the petitioner is among the small percentage at the very top of the field. The judging activity was routine peer review and local faculty review, not indicative of acclaim. The scholarly articles were mostly from his training years, not shown to have impact distinguishing him from other researchers, and his co-authorship of a new journal's first article had no presumption of distinction. Letters from colleagues attested to his skill but did not reflect national or international recognition; even a statement from the territorial Governor focused on a local physician shortage rather than specific medical contributions. The AAO emphasized that addressing a local shortage of qualified physicians is not tantamount to national or international acclaim, and that civic dedication and valuable skills — even at the major league level, per Matter of Price — do not automatically meet the extraordinary ability standard.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
United States v. Cunningham, 161 F.3d 1343 (11th Cir. 1998)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
USCIS Policy Memorandum PM 602-0005.1, Evaluation of Evidence Submitted with Certain Form I-140 Petitions (Dec. 22, 2010)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner abandoned the published material criterion (iii) by not contesting the Director's conclusion on appeal, per Matter of R-A-M-. The Director made an analytical error regarding the high salary criterion by improperly deriving a lower 'base salary' from pay receipts that subtracted paid leave from hours worked — the AAO corrected this and found the criterion satisfied. No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
